{{nihongo|'''Hakozaki Shrine'''|筥崎宮}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Chikuzen Province]].

'''Hakozaki-gū''' (はこざきぐう) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Hakozaki, Fukuoka|Q11603321|qid=Q11603321|lt=Hakozaki|1=Hakozaki}}, {{ill|lt=Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|Q1206659|qid=Q1206659|lt=Higashi Ward|1=Higashi-ku, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka|Q26600|qid=Q26600|1=Fukuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|Q123258|qid=Q123258|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and an {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Chikuzen Province|Q1047664|qid=Q1047664|1=Chikuzen Province}}{{Efn|Together with {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)|Q29682|qid=Q29682|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine (Fukuoka)}} in Sumiyoshi, {{ill|lt=Hakata-ku|Q1330839|qid=Q1330839|1=Hakata-ku, Fukuoka}}.}}. Its former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system#Government shrines|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, and it is now a {{ill|lt=Beppyō Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} under the {{ill|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.

It is also called '''Hakozaki Hachimangū''' (はこざきはちまんぐう). Together with {{ill|lt=Usa Jingū|Q715632|qid=Q715632|1=Usa Jingū}} in {{ill|lt=Usa, Ōita|Q114147|qid=Q114147|1=Usa, Ōita}}, {{ill|lt=Ōita Prefecture|Q133924|qid=Q133924|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, and {{ill|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Q710098|qid=Q710098|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} in {{ill|lt=Yawata, Kyoto|Q260923|qid=Q260923|1=Yawata}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|qid=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, it is called one of the '''{{ill|lt=Hachiman#Three Great Hachiman Shrines|Q261637|qid=Q261637|lt=Three Great Hachiman Shrines|1=Hachiman}}'''{{Efn|In popular usage, the “Three Great Hachiman” usually means “Usa Jingū and Iwashimizu Hachimangū,” plus either this shrine or {{ill|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Q701403|qid=Q701403|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}}. In late-Edo to Meiji materials, the three examples listed in the Daijō-kan notice dated old-calendar {{ill|lt=April 24, 1868|Q839640|qid=Q839640|1=April 24 (Lunisolar)}}<ref>{{Cite book |ja|author=Tanaka Tsunekiyo |title=Mysterious God: Everything about Hachiman, as told by the chief priest of Iwashimizu Hachimangū |publisher=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha |date=2010-03 |isbn=4404038291 |page=198}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~s_minaga/s_tatu.htm |title=Shinbutsu Bunri Order |website=Shinbutsu Bunri, or Haibutsu Kishaku |access-date=2015-12-25 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081030054401/https://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~s_minaga/s_tatu.htm |archive-date=2008-10-30}}{{信頼性要検証|date=2024-11}}</ref> and ranked as Kanpei Taisha were “Usa, Iwashimizu, Hakozaki”; however, some recent books present “Usa, Iwashimizu, Tsurugaoka” as representative Hachiman shrines<ref>{{Cite book |ja |title=National Directory of Hachiman Shrines |publisher=Shin Jinbutsu Ōraisha |series=Bessatsu Rekishi Dokuhon 99 Shrine Series 3| date=2004-11 |isbn=4404030991 |page=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |ja |author=Shirai Eiji |author2=Toki Masanori |title=Shrine Dictionary |edition=New popular edition |publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan |date=1997-09 |isbn=449010474X |page=}}</ref>.}}.

=== Shrine name ===
The proper character for “hako” is 「筥」 (a cylindrical container), not 「箱」. However, for place names, station names, etc., {{要出典範囲|the toponym “hakozaki” is written as 「箱崎」 rather than the shrine’s 「筥崎」 out of reverence for the Hakozaki Hachiman deity.|date=2023-03}}

<gallery>
Japanese Crest mitu Tomoe 1.svg|Hakozaki-gū crest {{ill|lt=tomoe|Q1557435|qid=Q1557435|lt=(left triple tomoe)|1=Tomoe}}
</gallery>

=== History ===
[[File:箱崎八幡宮縁起.jpg|thumb|left|350px|Origin account of Hakozaki Hachimangū]]

There are multiple theories regarding its founding<ref name="history">{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/history/ |title=History of Hakozaki-gū |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref><ref name="tsuchida">{{Cite journal |ja |author=Mitsuyoshi Tsuchida |year=1972 |title=On the architecture of the main sanctuary of Hakozaki Hachimangū |journal=Transactions of the Architectural Institute of Japan |volume=195 |pages=69-74,98 |publisher=Architectural Institute of Japan |crid=1390001205537700736| doi=10.3130/aijsaxx.195.0_69}}</ref>; the official site introduces the theory that it was established in {{ill|lt=Engi|Q1374325|qid=Q1374325|1=Engi (era)}} 21<ref name="history" />. On June 21 of Engi 21 ({{ill|lt=921|Q30102|qid=Q30102|1=921}}), there was an oracle from {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|qid=Q261637|1=Hachiman}}, and it began when Ōjin, Jingū, and Tamayori-hime were enshrined by relocating {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Daibumiya|1=UNKNOWN}} in {{ill|lt=Honami District|Q11597062|qid=Q11597062|1=Honami district}}, Chikuzen Province, to land facing the {{ill|lt=Genkai Sea|Q498047|qid=Q498047|1=Genkai Sea}}<ref name="history" />. In {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|qid=Q1327274|lt=Enchō|1=Enchō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=923|Q30108|qid=Q30108|1=923}}), it was transferred to the current site<ref name="history" />. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' it is listed as “Hachiman Daibosatsu Hakozaki-gū, one seat,” and ranked as a {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}.

During the {{ill|lt=Mongol invasions of Japan|Q208156|qid=Q208156|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}}, retired emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Kameyama|Q360972|qid=Q360972|lt=Kameyama Jōkō|1=Emperor Kameyama}} prayed for the enemy’s submission; a copy of his writing was made in the {{ill|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|Q319531|qid=Q319531|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} and displayed on the shrine gate as a {{ill|lt=hengaku|Q5358873|qid=Q5358873|1=biane}} reading “敵國降伏” (submission of enemy countries)<ref name="history" />. Since then, it has been worshipped as a deity of maritime traffic and overseas defense.

Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine in Meiji 4 ({{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}}). It was promoted to Kanpei Chūsha in {{ill|lt=1885|Q7821|qid=Q7821|1=1885}} (Meiji 18), and to Kanpei Taisha in {{ill|lt=1914|Q2083|qid=Q2083|1=1914}} (Taishō 3)<ref name="history" />.

=== Precincts ===
The main buildings and facilities in the precincts are as follows.
* Main sanctuary and worship hall
** Important Cultural Property. Founded in June 921 (Engi 21) by Fujiwara no Mazai. Repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt multiple times<ref name="tsuchida" />. It fell into ruin due to warfare in the {{ill|lt=Sengoku period|Q204023|qid=Q204023|1=Sengoku period}}, and was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} 15 (1546) by Dazai Daini {{ill|lt=Ōuchi Yoshitaka|Q1065163|qid=Q1065163|1=Ōuchi Yoshitaka}}<ref name="tsuchida" />. The main sanctuary is 46 tsubo, {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=hiwada-buki|1=UNKNOWN}} roof, with side carriage porches<ref name="keidai">{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/keidai/ |title=Guide to the precincts |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>. The worship hall is {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|qid=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} with cypress bark roofing<ref name="keidai" />, double {{ill|lt=kōryō|Q124421222|qid=Q124421222|1=kōryō}}, and {{ill|lt=kamamata|Q123117911|qid=Q123117911|1=kaerumata}}.
* Rōmon gate
** Important Cultural Property. Built in {{ill|lt=Bunroku|Q1373039|qid=Q1373039|1=Bunroku}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1594|Q6777|qid=Q6777|1=1594}}) by {{ill|lt=Kobayakawa Takakage|Q1143508|qid=Q1143508|1=Kobayakawa Takakage}}<ref name="keidai" />. Three-bay, one-door {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|qid=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}}<ref name="keidai" />. The paulownia crest carving on the doors is said to be by {{ill|lt=Hidari Jingorō|Q2668220|qid=Q2668220|1=Hidari Jingorō}}<ref name="keidai" />. The “Enemy Country Submission” {{ill|lt=hengaku|Q5358873|qid=Q5358873|1=biane}} is said to be an imperial inscription by {{ill|lt=Emperor Daigo|Q329753|qid=Q329753|1=Emperor Daigo}} donated by {{ill|lt=Kameyama Jōkō|Q360972|qid=Q360972|1=Emperor Kameyama}}. “Fukuteki-mon” in {{ill|lt=Hirose Tansō|Q1620267|qid=Q1620267|1=Hirose Tansō}}’s poem “Chikuzen Jōka-saku” refers to this gate. General worship is performed under this rōmon. It is counted as one of Japan’s three great rōmon.
* Stone First Torii (Hizen Torii)
** Important Cultural Property. Long said to have been erected in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|qid=Q6762|1=1609}}) by {{ill|lt=Kuroda Nagamasa|Q950496|qid=Q950496|1=Kuroda Nagamasa}}<ref name="keidai" />, but Sasebo City cultural property examiner Hisamura Sadao established that it was erected by {{ill|lt=Saga Domain|Q2975995|qid=Q2975995|1=Saga Domain}} lord {{ill|lt=Nabeshima Katsushige|Q3138980|qid=Q3138980|1=Nabeshima Katsushige}}<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=https://www.saga-s.co.jp/articles/-/1008063 |title=Returning a favor to Kuroda Nagamasa in “Sekigahara” links: National Important Cultural Property “Hakozaki Torii” may have been donated by the Nabeshima clan {{!}} Administration/Society {{!}} Saga Shimbun News |website=Saga Shimbun |publisher=Saga Shimbunsha |date=2024-05-08 |access-date=2024-05-08}}</ref>. The torii pillars are made in three sections, bulging downward and continuing into base stones<ref name="keidai" />. The kasagi and shimaki are one stone piece, with upturned tips, and equal lengths of nuki and kasagi—typical '''Hizen torii'''<ref name="keidai" />. The stone used is {{ill|lt=Aō stone|Q109360266|qid=Q109360266|1=Aō stone}}, a specialty of {{ill|lt=Takashima, Nagasaki|Q10860535|qid=Q10860535|lt=Takashima|1=Takashima}}, {{ill|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|Q169376|qid=Q169376|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=https://tabinaga.jp/column/view.php?category=5&hid=20140226202035 |author=Moriki Matsu |title=The stone industry tradition of Takashima: “Aō stone” |website=Traveling Nagasaki Studies |publisher=Nagasaki Prefectural Cultural Promotion Division |access-date=2021-05-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210506120926/https://tabinaga.jp/column/view.php?category=5&hid=20140226202035 |archive-date=2021-05-06}}</ref>. Calling it “Hakozaki torii” is incorrect.
* Stone lantern
** Important Cultural Property. Of many stone lanterns, one with an inscription dated {{ill|lt=Kanno|Q1145053|qid=Q1145053|1=Kannō}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1350|Q6094|qid=Q6094|1=1350}}) on the underside of the fire box is said to have been donated by {{ill|lt=Sen no Rikyū|Q305477|qid=Q305477|1=Sen no Rikyū}} in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1587|Q6744|qid=Q6744|1=1587}}).
* Sacred tree “Hako-matsu”
** A pine tree on the right of the rōmon enclosed by a red {{ill|lt=tamagaki|Q3078312|qid=Q3078312|1=Sacred fences}} fence<ref name="keidai" />. It is said that when {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|qid=Q232803|1=Empress Jingū}} gave birth to {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|qid=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, the {{ill|lt=placenta|Q1212935|qid=Q1212935|lt=ena|1=Placenta}} was put in a box and buried, and this sacred pine was planted as a marker<ref name="keidai" />. The name “Hakozaki” derives from that box containing the placenta<ref name="keidai" />.
* Karafune-tō
* Yūshutsu-seki
* Shrine garden Hanateien{{Efn|Location: Maidashi 4-chome, external link: <ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=https://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/hanameguri/ |title=Approach flower tour |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2022-11-21}}</ref>}}
* {{読み仮名|Tall Lantern|たかとうろう}}{{Efn|Location: Hakozaki 2-chome 55 {{Coord|33|37|1.86|N|130|25|0.17|E}}. Summary of local signboard: “A stone lantern about 6 meters tall. First built in {{ill|lt=1817|Q6958|qid=Q6958|1=1817}} (Bunka 14) by fishermen of Hakozaki-ura as a landmark for returning from fishing in {{ill|lt=Hakata Bay|Q2344806|qid=Q2344806|1=Hakata Bay}}. In {{ill|lt=1882|Q7812|qid=Q7812|1=1882}} (Meiji 15) it was moved to the present location, then at the shoreline, and its upper part was changed from wood to stone. It was repaired again in {{ill|lt=1968|Q2644|qid=Q2644|1=1968}} (Shōwa 43).”<ref>{{Cite report |df=ja |author=Fukuoka City Higashi Ward Office / Hakozaki school district council |title=Tall Lantern |location=Hakozaki 2-chome 55 |at=Signboard (onsite)}}</ref>. External link: <ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=https://bunkazai.city.fukuoka.lg.jp/ |title=Fukuoka City Cultural Properties |publisher=Fukuoka City Bureau of Economy, Tourism and Culture, Cultural Properties Utilization Division |access-date=2022-11-22}} → cultural property search → keyword search</ref>}}
* {{読み仮名|Hakozaki-gū Hamamiya|はこざきぐうはまみや}}{{Efn|Location: Hakozaki 2-chome 55 {{Coord|33|37|2.06|N|130|25|2.01|E}}, alias: traffic safety prayer hall (large vehicles)}}

Also, a long approach of about 850 meters continues from the first torii facing {{ill|lt=Hakata Bay|Q2344806|qid=Q2344806|1=Hakata Bay}} to the main sanctuary. The Hakozaki-hama area in front of the approach formerly had a beautiful {{ill|lt=coastline|Q93352|qid=Q93352|lt=coast|1=Coast}} praised as {{ill|lt=white sands and green pines|Q11580253|qid=Q11580253|1=white sands and green pines}}, but harbor works for Hakata Port brought seawall development by {{ill|lt=1936|Q18649|qid=Q18649|1=1936}}, and today the area has changed drastically, with {{ill|lt=Hakozaki Wharf|Q110985033|qid=Q110985033|1=Hakozaki Futō}} to the north and {{ill|lt=Higashihama, Fukuoka|Q110970101|qid=Q110970101|lt=Higashihama|1=Higashihama}} (Higashihama Wharf) and Hakata Port warehouses to the south. The beach beyond the approach is maintained as sandy ground so people can perform “Oshioi-tori,” receiving purifying {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=masago|Q3275386|qid=Q3275386|lt=sand|1=Decomposed granite}}|まさご}}. Hakozaki-hama is adjacent to Hakozaki boat basin{{Efn|Hakozaki boat basin is not a {{ill|lt=fishing port|Q1420027|qid=Q1420027|1=fishing port}} designated under the {{ill|lt=Fishing Port and Fishing Grounds Improvement Act|Q11565358|qid=Q11565358|1=Act on Development of Fishing Ports and Grounds}}; it corresponds to “{{ill|lt=boat basin|Q106835724|qid=Q106835724|1=basin for small craft}},” one of the water-area facilities defined in Article 2, Paragraph 5, Item 1 of the Port and Harbor Act. One corner also has the {{ill|lt=Hakozaki branch|Q11592233|qid=Q11592233|lt=Hakozaki Branch|1=Fukuoka Fisheries Cooperative}} of the {{ill|lt=Fukuoka City Fisheries Cooperative|Q11592233|qid=Q11592233|1=Fukuoka Fisheries Cooperative}}.}}. Besides the first torii in front of the main hall, there is a second torii near the intersection of the approach and the Nishitetsu bus-only road (Nishitetsu Hakozaki bus stop / Subway Hakozaki-miyamae Station Exit 1). A large torii built in 1930 once stood at the intersection of the seaward {{ill|lt=National Route 3|Q1138454|qid=Q1138454|1=Japan National Route 3}} and the approach, but it was dismantled and removed between March and April 2018 due to risk of mortar detachment from deterioration.

=== Major festivals ===
* January 3: Tamaseseri (Ball-Grabbing Festival)<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/tamatorisai/ |title=Tamaseseri |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
** Men in {{ill|lt=fundoshi|Q976963|qid=Q976963|lt=shimekomi|1=Fundoshi}} attire struggle over a wooden sacred jewel (said to grant good fortune) and offer it into the main hall.
* January 11: Jōtenji Ichizan Hōsaishiki<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/jotenjiichizanhousaishiki/ |title=Jōtenji Ichizan Hōsaishiki |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
** Monks of {{ill|lt=Jōten-ji|Q3191242|qid=Q3191242|1=Jōten-ji}} chant sutras before the deity in gratitude, because Shōichi Kokushi {{ill|lt=Enni|Q739826|qid=Q739826|1=Enni}} (Ben’en), when returning from Song China, survived maritime danger by protection of Hakozaki Hachiman.
* Day of Tsuchinoe nearest spring equinox: Spring Shani-sai (Oshioi-tori)<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/shunkishanichisai/ |title=Spring Shani-sai / Oshioi-tori |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
** Purifying sand is received at Hakozaki-hama and scattered at homes and fields for household safety and good harvest.
* Late June: Ikeshima-dono Grand Festival<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/ikeshimadensai/ |title=Ikeshima-dono Festival |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
* July 1 and 9: Oshioi-tori
** Carriers of the {{ill|lt=Hakata Gion Yamakasa|Q1749262|qid=Q1749262|1=Hakata Gion Yamakasa}} take sand at Hakozaki-hama for purification.
* July 7: Tanabata Festival<ref>{{Cite web |ja|url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/tanabatasai/ |title=Tanabata Festival |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
* Late July: Nagoshi Festival<ref>{{Cite web |ja|url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/nagoshisai/ |title=Nagoshi Festival / Chinowa passage |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
* September 12–18: {{読み仮名|{{ill|lt=Hōjō-e (Hakozaki-gū)|Q67704099|qid=Q67704099|lt=Hōjō-e|1=Hōjōya}}|ほうじょうや}}<ref name="houjoya">{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/houjoya/ |title=Hōjō-e |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
** Festival honoring life and giving thanks for blessings of sea and mountains<ref name="houjoya" />. Considered one of Hakata’s three great festivals<ref name="houjoya" />. Unglazed-clay {{ill|lt=ohajiki|Q11262088|qid=Q11262088|1=Ohajiki}} and glass noisemaker toys “chanpon” ({{ill|lt=popin|Q11278090|qid=Q11278090|lt=bidoro|1=bidoro}}, {{ill|lt=popin|Q11278090|qid=Q11278090|lt=poppen|1=poppen}}) are sold. Various stalls line the 1-{{ill|lt=kilometre|Q828224|qid=Q828224|1=Kilometre}} approach. In Hakata it is affectionately called “pear and persimmon season is Hōjō-e.” {{要出典範囲|date=2017-09|Generally “放生会” is read “hōjō'''e'''”, but}} at Hakozaki-gū and especially in Fukuoka it is traditionally read “hōjō'''ya'''” to distinguish it from Buddhist events read “hōjōe”<ref name="houjoya" />.
* Day of Tsuchinoe nearest autumn equinox: Autumn Shani-sai (Oshioi-tori)<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/shukishanichisai/ |title=Autumn Shani-sai / Oshioi-tori |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>
* December 31: Oena Festival (Great Purification, sea-cucumber mochi pounding)<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=http://www.hakozakigu.or.jp/omatsuri/ |title=Festivals |publisher=Hakozaki-gū |access-date=2017-09-09}}</ref>

=== Cultural properties ===
=== Important Cultural Properties (national) ===
* Main sanctuary - Built in late Muromachi period (1545). Nine-bay nagare-zukuri, cypress bark roof. Designated May 27, 1907.
* Worship hall - Built around late Muromachi period (1545). Four bays wide, one bay deep, single-story, kirizuma-zukuri, cypress bark roof. Designated May 27, 1907.
* Rōmon gate - Built in Azuchi–Momoyama period (1573–1614). Three-bay one-door rōmon, irimoya-zukuri, cypress bark roof. Designated April 17, 1902.
* First torii - Built in Azuchi–Momoyama period (1609). Stone myōjin torii. Pillars bear inscription “Keichō 14, year of 己酉, mid-autumn.” Designated June 22, 1955.
* Stone lantern - Built in Nanboku-chō period (1350). Designated February 2, 1962.

* Tachi, attributed {{ill|lt=Sanjō Munechika|Q11355828|qid=Q11355828|lt=Munechika|1=Sanjō Munechika}}
* Sword inscribed “Resident source Saemon-no-jō in precincts of Chikuzen Hakozaki Hachimangū”
* Key

* Bronze bowl
* Tanka slips and kaishi paper
* Hakozaki-gū documents

=== Imperial-plaque postage stamp of Hakozaki-gū ===
{{double image aside|right|Tejikoku10sen.JPG|120|Inperf 10sen stamp in 1945.JPG|110|Imperial-plaque stamp (1945)<br/>There were perforated (left) and imperforate (right) versions.}}
{{main|Tekikoku Kōfuku stamp}}
As noted above, during the Mongol invasions {{ill|lt=Kameyama Jōkō|Q360972|qid=Q360972|1=Emperor Kameyama}} prayed for “submission of enemy countries,” and that plaque hangs on the rōmon. In {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|qid=Q5240|1=1945}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 20), late in the {{ill|lt=Greater East Asia War|Q3846875|qid=Q3846875|1=Greater East Asia War}} ({{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|qid=Q362|1=World War II}}), the {{ill|lt=Teishin-in|Q11639479|qid=Q11639479|1=Ministry of Communications (Japan)}} (now {{ill|lt=Japan Post|Q11509260|qid=Q11509260|1=Japan Post Service}}) issued a {{ill|lt=definitive stamp|Q1002873|qid=Q1002873|1=Definitive stamp}} using this plaque design to boost morale and pray for victory. Due to material shortages it was printed in black only, and imperforate versions were also made. However, before it reached post offices nationwide, the war ended, and in {{ill|lt=1947|Q5263|qid=Q5263|1=1947}} (Shōwa 22), the “Ordinance prohibiting use of postage stamps and postcards whose designs relate to militarism, Shinto, etc.” (1947 Teishin Ministry Ordinance No. 24<ref>{{Cite web |ja |url=https://elaws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=322M40001000024 |title=Ordinance prohibiting use of stamps and postcards whose designs relate to militarism, Shinto, etc. |website=e-Gov law search |access-date=2021-04-21}}</ref>; an ordinance on {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=purged stamps|1=UNKNOWN}}) banned it from postal use. Even as of {{ill|lt=2021|Q49628|qid=Q49628|1=2021}} (Reiwa 3), this stamp can still be exchanged for current stamps/postcards at {{ill|lt=Tokyo Central Post Office|Q5153849|qid=Q5153849|1=Tokyo Central Post Office}} in {{ill|lt=Chiyoda, Tokyo|Q214051|qid=Q214051|1=Chiyoda, Tokyo}}, {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|qid=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.
{{See also|Definitive stamps of Japan#Validity of Japanese stamps|Japanese postage stamps#Overview}}
